title: Government AI Readiness Tracker
description: A government AI readiness tracker scoring cities, states, regions and national programmes on 24 dimensions (0/1/2: none/announced/deployed) across six groups, with per-claim primary-source citations and verification dates. Covers operational evidence, not just strategy documents.
citation: Vorp Labs Research, "Government AI Readiness Tracker," vorplabs.com, 2026.

scoreScale.0: No qualifying evidence after the documented search, or an explicit official statement that the capability does not exist.
scoreScale.1: Strategy commitment, funded plan, procurement, pilot, proof of concept or development project without production-use evidence.
scoreScale.2: Live register, system or binding process currently in force, with an operational as-of date.

dimensions.label: AI/digital strategy
dimensions.description: Approved and currently valid strategy.

dimensions.label: Dedicated AI governance
dimensions.description: Named office, board or accountable role operating now.

dimensions.label: Staffing and budget
dimensions.description: Current headcount or budget assigned to AI governance/delivery.

dimensions.label: Internal AI/system inventory
dimensions.description: Organization-wide inventory used for governance.

dimensions.label: Public algorithm/AI register
dimensions.description: Searchable public register with live entries.

dimensions.label: Register completeness
dimensions.description: Reconciliation against internal inventory.

dimensions.label: Transparency and labeling
dimensions.description: AI interaction/content notices operational.

dimensions.label: Independent evaluation/audit
dimensions.description: Audit, academic or external evaluation completed.

dimensions.label: Public-facing AI deployed
dimensions.description: AI used in resident-facing services.

dimensions.label: Employee/internal AI deployed
dimensions.description: Internal assistant, coding tool, search or automation.

dimensions.label: Open source/interoperability
dimensions.description: Released code, reusable standards or documented APIs.

dimensions.label: Adoption and outcome metrics
dimensions.description: Current usage plus service/outcome measures.

dimensions.label: AI literacy and training
dimensions.description: Required or measured training program in operation.

dimensions.label: Procurement guidance
dimensions.description: Binding AI clauses, model terms or supplier requirements.

dimensions.label: Data and technical infrastructure
dimensions.description: Operational secure hosting, model gateway, compute or data platform.

dimensions.label: Sandbox/pilot participation
dimensions.description: Actual participation, not a policy aspiration.

dimensions.label: Risk classification
dimensions.description: Repeatable AI Act or local risk-tiering process in force.

dimensions.label: AI Act obligation inventory
dimensions.description: Mapped provider/deployer roles and applicable articles.

dimensions.label: Article 49 registration readiness
dimensions.description: EU database registration workflow and links.

dimensions.label: FRIA/DPIA/impact assessment
dimensions.description: Assessments completed before applicable deployment.

dimensions.label: Human oversight and appeal
dimensions.description: Named human-control and challenge/redress process.

dimensions.label: Monitoring and incident management
dimensions.description: Performance monitoring, complaint and incident process.

dimensions.label: Public participation
dimensions.description: Feedback or consultation changes system governance.

dimensions.label: Freshness discipline
dimensions.description: Register and policy records demonstrably maintained.

entries.editorialSummary: Register-first path: public disclosure since 2020, then lifecycle governance, procurement clauses, impact assessments and internal GenAI. High transparency ambition, but register coverage is still being completed and the 2024 audit found governance gaps.

dedicated-governance.note: Expanded central algorithm team; exact 2026 directorate not publicly identified.

staffing-budget.note: Central algorithm team expanded per 2025 progress report; headcount not published.

internal-inventory.note: 2024–2026 plan targets full high-risk and limited-risk analysis; completion not proven by the 69-entry snapshot.

public-register.note: 69 published descriptions in the Dutch national register as of 2026-07-20. Includes in-use, in-development and out-of-use entries.

register-completeness.note: No public reconciliation against internal inventory.

transparency-labeling.note: ChatAmsterdam record lists DPIA and ethical assessment; no broad public AI interaction notices verified.

independent-evaluation.note: Amsterdam Rekenkamer 2024 audit found governance weaknesses; remediation program underway.

public-facing-ai.note: Register includes computer-vision and decision-support systems used in city services; specific resident-facing AI not individually verified beyond register.

employee-ai.note: ChatAmsterdam (internal GenAI, begin date 2024-11, marked in use).

open-source.note: Algorithmic procurement model provisions published; code not released as open source.

adoption-metrics.note: 69 register entries; no user/session metrics for ChatAmsterdam published.

procurement-guidance.note: Contractual information requirements for suppliers of algorithmic applications adopted November 2020.

risk-classification.note: 2024–2026 plan includes analyzing high- and limited-risk algorithms; completion not proven.

obligation-inventory.note: AI4Citizens work updating governance instruments for AI Act requirements.

fria-dpia.note: IAMA (Impact Assessment Mensenrechten en Algoritmes) in use; DPIA evidence in ChatAmsterdam record.

human-oversight.note: System contacts listed in register; no public appeal/redress process verified.

monitoring-incidents.note: No public incident log or incident process verified.

public-participation.note: Readability review by communications directorate; no structured public feedback mechanism verified.

freshness-discipline.note: Register entries show mixed lifecycle status and dates; 2025 progress report confirms ongoing maintenance.

keyFindings: 69 published descriptions in the Dutch national register as of 2026-07-20, tied with the Dutch Tax Administration.
ChatAmsterdam (internal GenAI, begin date 2024-11) is recorded as in use.
Amsterdam Rekenkamer 2024 audit found governance instruments were a patchwork with unclear relationships.
Register coverage is still being completed under the 2024–2026 remediation program.

sources.note: Municipal lifecycle governance playbook.

sources.note: 69 Amsterdam entries in the Dutch register.

sources.label: Amsterdam Rekenkamer 2024 audit (Onderzoeksrapport Algoritmen)
sources.note: Independent public audit of algorithm governance.

sources.label: 2025 progress report (Plan van Aanpak Algoritmes)
sources.note: Current remediation program status.

sources.label: ChatAmsterdam register entry
sources.note: Internal GenAI platform, marked in use.

sources.label: IAMA (Impact Assessment Mensenrechten en Algoritmes)
sources.note: Human-rights assessment instrument.

sources.label: Algorithmic procurement model provisions
sources.note: Supplier information requirements for algorithmic systems.

sources.label: Amsterdam and Helsinki launch open AI register (2020-09-28)
sources.note: Joint launch announcement.

entries.editorialSummary: Public AI-service catalog/register-first path, co-developed with Amsterdam. Emphasizes understandable service cards, ethical principles and lifecycle transparency (retired systems remain visible). The Finnish register page has 18 cards; the English version shows only 9, a material language-sync gap.

ai-strategy.note: Data strategy, ethical principles confirmed in 2023 and 2026 communications AI principles in force.

dedicated-governance.note: City Executive Office oversees the register; public contact ai@hel.fi.

internal-inventory.note: Register is continually supplemented but no completeness percentage or internal inventory reconciliation published.

public-register.note: 18 cards on Finnish page (14 current/test, 4 retired); 9 cards on English page. Finnish page is canonical.

register-completeness.note: No authoritative internal denominator published; completeness not independently demonstrated.

transparency-labeling.note: 2026 communications principles require disclosure of AI-generated content; system-level interaction notices not individually verified.

independent-evaluation.note: Academic analyses cite Helsinki as an example (Cath & Jansen 2021; stabilizing-translucencies study); no commissioned city audit located.

public-facing-ai.note: Helsinki Pathfinder, advisory videos and other tools listed on the register.

employee-ai.note: DuckDuckGo AI Chat and Adobe Creative Cloud generative functions listed; M365 Copilot trial documented as no longer in use.

open-source.note: Public AI Registers white paper and documentation framework published; no code release verified.

ai-literacy.note: 2026 communications principles include AI-literacy controls; program-wide training scope not verified.

human-oversight.note: Ethical principles require a responsible party for each AI-enabled service whom customers can contact, but no uniform challenge or redress route was verified.

monitoring-incidents.note: No public incident register or incident process located.

public-participation.note: Register includes system-level description fields; no structured feedback mechanism verified.

freshness-discipline.note: Retired systems remain visible with dates (positive), but Finnish/English mismatch shows maintenance gaps.

keyFindings: 18 cards on the Finnish register page; only 9 on the English version: a material language-sync gap.
4 of 18 cards are explicitly discontinued or not currently in use: retirement transparency is a positive maturity signal.
Co-developed the public AI register model with Amsterdam, launched 2020-09-28.
2026 communications principles require disclosure of AI-generated content and human verification.

sources.label: Helsingin kaupungin tekoälyrekisteri (Finnish register)
sources.note: 18 cards, canonical version.

sources.label: City of Helsinki AI Register (English)
sources.note: 9 cards; lags Finnish version.

sources.label: Public AI Registers white paper
sources.note: Joint Helsinki/Amsterdam documentation framework.

sources.label: City decision on ethical principles for data and AI
sources.note: Official 2023 decision adopting eight ethical principles.

sources.label: Principles for AI in city communications (2026)
sources.note: AI disclosure and verification requirements.

sources.label: Helsinki and Amsterdam launch open AI register
sources.note: Joint launch announcement, 2020-09-28.

entries.editorialSummary: Strategy-and-tool-first path: AI strategy v2.1 (2024-10-24), WienKI internal GenAI platform with over 2,500 active users (as of 2025-11-27), AI Compass v3.0 employee guidelines and a CKT risk-compliance framework. Strong evidenced internal deployment, but no public register or public citywide AI inventory verified.

ai-strategy.note: KI-Strategie der Stadt Wien v2.1, valid from 2024-10-24; replaces 2019 strategy.

dedicated-governance.note: MD-OS/PIKT (Magistratsdirektion, Prozessmanagement und IKT-Strategie) owns the strategy; CIO role identified.

staffing-budget.note: 14 employees in MD-OS/PIKT handle central ICT steering including AI (2026-04-23 mayoral response); this is not 14 dedicated AI staff.

internal-inventory.note: Strategy calls for a central electronic directory; no public proof of operational coverage found.

public-register.note: No public citywide algorithm register or AI inventory verified.

register-completeness.note: No public register exists to reconcile.

transparency-labeling.note: AI Compass v3.0 requires disclosure controls; no resident-facing AI interaction notices verified.

independent-evaluation.note: No independent academic or audit evaluation of WienKI’s outcomes, error rates or rights impacts located.

public-facing-ai.note: WienKI described as a base for future citizen processes; no named citizen-facing AI service operationally evidenced.

employee-ai.note: WienKI: 2,500+ active users, 70,000 enabled accounts, 104,000 chats (self-reported as of 2025-11-27). Enabled accounts ≠ active users.

adoption-metrics.note: Detailed self-reported metrics: active users, enabled accounts, chats, tokens, documents, community members. Dated 2025-11-27.

ai-literacy.note: AI Compass v3.0 mandates AI and fake-news e-learning; employee-use controls in force since 2025-07-01.

procurement-guidance.note: CKT process covers provider/service assessment; no binding published procurement clauses verified.

data-infrastructure.note: WienKI is operated by Wien Digital (MA 01); Red Hat Innovation Award 2025 confirms production infrastructure.

risk-classification.note: CKT (Cloud and AI Compliance) process uses a two-phase risk-based review publicly documented since 2026-03-19.

obligation-inventory.note: Strategy v2.1 references AI Act compliance; completion of Article-level mapping not proven.

fria-dpia.note: CKT process includes privacy assessment; no completed FRIA or public DPIA summary located.

human-oversight.note: AI Compass mandates human responsibility and the CKT process requires human approval, but no uniform challenge or redress route was verified.

monitoring-incidents.note: No public incident log or process verified.

public-participation.note: AI expert pool continued through 2025–2026; no structured citizen feedback mechanism verified.

freshness-discipline.note: Strategy v2.1 and Compass v3.0 show updates; WienKI metrics are 8 months old as of the verification date.

keyFindings: WienKI: 2,500+ active users and 104,000 chats (self-reported, 2025-11-27). Enabled accounts (70,000) must not be conflated with active users.
No public algorithm register or citywide AI inventory despite the strategy promising one.
AI Compass v3.0 (effective 2025-07-01) mandates e-learning, disclosure controls and human responsibility.
CKT risk-compliance process publicly documented since 2026-03-19.
Awards (Red Hat Innovation Award 2025, EU Public Sector Tech Watch 2025, Digital Humanism 2026) are recognition, not independent effectiveness evidence.

sources.note: Valid from 2024-10-24.

sources.label: Wien veröffentlicht neue KI-Strategie (launch)
sources.note: Strategy v2.1 announcement.

sources.label: WienKI usage metrics (2025-11-27 press release)
sources.note: Self-reported active users, chats and enabled accounts.

sources.label: KI-Rahmenbedingungen (CKT compliance framework)
sources.note: Two-phase Cloud and AI Compliance process, published 2026-03-19.

sources.label: AI Compass v3.0 (KI-Kompass)
sources.note: Employee AI use guidelines, effective 2025-07-01.

sources.label: Mayoral response on AI staffing (2026-04-23)
sources.note: 14 employees in MD-OS/PIKT handling ICT steering including AI.

sources.label: Stadt Wien gewinnt Red Hat Innovation Award 2025
sources.note: Award recognition; not independent effectiveness evidence.

entries.editorialSummary: Aggregation-and-standardization path: the broadest countable transparency infrastructure with 1,509 algorithm descriptions from 349 organizations. Structured dashboards, organization maps and bulk downloads. Remains voluntary and incomplete; not the EU Article 71 database.

ai-strategy.note: Algorithm Framework (wider laws, standards, measures and tools) in place.

dedicated-governance.note: Ministry of the Interior and Kingdom Relations (BZK) operates the register; DPA acts as coordinating Algorithm Supervisor.

staffing-budget.note: Exact product-owner unit within BZK not publicly identified.

internal-inventory.note: The national register itself functions as a structured multi-agency inventory.

public-register.note: 1,509 algorithm descriptions from 349 organizations as of 2026-07-20.

register-completeness.note: Official site states publication is incomplete and not generally mandatory.

transparency-labeling.note: Register descriptions include purpose and data processing; no resident-facing interaction notices verified.

independent-evaluation.note: Nieuwenhuizen 2025 study (27 interviews, 33 policy documents) examined the register’s transparency value.

public-facing-ai.note: The register aggregates use cases across agencies; individual system deployment is per-organization.

open-source.note: Register data available via bulk download; API and organization browsing publicly accessible.

adoption-metrics.note: 1,509 descriptions / 349 organizations with dashboards and organization-level counts.

data-infrastructure.note: National platform with search, dashboards, organization maps, download and submission functions.

risk-classification.note: Register includes impactful algorithms but not restricted to high-risk AI; no systematic risk classification visible.

obligation-inventory.note: 2026 IAMA updated for Article 27 alignment; EU database interoperability not publicly evidenced.

article-49-readiness.note: National register is not the EU Article 71 database; interoperability not publicly evidenced.

fria-dpia.note: IAMA updated 2026-02-16 for Article 27 alignment; in use across Dutch public administration.

public-participation.note: Open data, target-group analysis used for product improvement; open onboarding for organizations.

freshness-discipline.note: Release notes document ongoing platform improvements through 2025–2026.

keyFindings: 1,509 algorithm descriptions from 349 organizations as of 2026-07-20.
Publication is voluntary and the register is not yet generally mandatory.
The register is broader than the EU AI Act database: it covers impactful algorithms, not only high-risk AI.
Largest publishers: Ministry of Finance (135), Tax Administration (69), Amsterdam (69), Customs (48), Utrecht (45), The Hague (44), Rotterdam (29).
IAMA updated 2026-02-16 for Article 27 alignment; a bridge to AI Act deployer compliance.

sources.label: Het Algoritmeregister van de Nederlandse overheid (Dutch)
sources.note: National register homepage.

sources.label: Organisatieoverzicht (organization overview)
sources.note: Per-organization description counts.

sources.label: Over het Algoritmeregister (about page, Frisian)
sources.note: States publication is not yet mandatory.

sources.label: Releasenotes (release notes)
sources.note: Ongoing platform improvements.

sources.label: IAMA 2026 update
sources.note: Updated for AI Act Article 27 alignment.

sources.label: Overheid gaat meer doen om problemen algoritmen te voorkomen (2022 announcement)
sources.note: Register launch announcement.

entries.officialName: Eesti Riigi Infosüsteemi Amet (RIA) / eesti.ai programme
entries.editorialSummary: Service-orchestration and digital-state path: strong e-government foundation and active AI programme (Bürokratt citizen virtual assistant, Aruait secure-assistant innovation project, Kratid capacity-building). Demonstrates technical capability, but public evidence is fragmented and lacks a current countable inventory. Weakest-verified of the original European cohort.

ai-strategy.note: National AI Strategy/Kratt programme exists but current authoritative strategy document and eesti.ai portal continuity were not freshness-verified.

dedicated-governance.note: RIA leads AI programme (confirmed by updated page 2026-06-19); Kratid support portfolio active.

internal-inventory.note: No current public total for systems, agencies or users located.

independent-evaluation.note: No current independent academic evaluation of Bürokratt’s coverage, accuracy or rights impacts located.

public-facing-ai.note: Bürokratt: citizen virtual assistant deployed at agency level (Statistics Estonia, Consumer Protection and Technical Regulatory Authority confirmed in official study).

employee-ai.note: Aruait is developing secure autonomous public-sector AI assistants; not yet evidenced as production-wide.

open-source.note: Bürokratt components are stated as open source; freshness of the repository not verified.

adoption-metrics.note: No current user/conversation/agency counts located. The 51-initiatives figure is from 2021 and must not be used as a 2026 count.

ai-literacy.note: Kratid offers workshops and AI-project support; scope not verified.

data-infrastructure.note: Estonia’s e-government stack (X-Road, e-identity) provides the underlying infrastructure.

human-oversight.note: Bürokratt described as able to hand off to a customer-service representative; no formal redress process verified.

monitoring-incidents.note: RIA AI page foregrounds cybersecurity analysis but no public incident register located.

freshness-discipline.note: RIA AI page updated 2026-06-19; eesti.ai portal continuity and Bürokratt launch chronology not freshness-verified.

keyFindings: Bürokratt is deployed at agency level as a citizen virtual assistant (Statistics Estonia, Consumer Protection and Technical Regulatory Authority confirmed).
Aruait is a newer innovation project developing secure autonomous public-sector AI assistants (not yet production-wide).
No current public total for systems, agencies or users located: weakest-verified of the original European cohort.
Strong e-government foundation (X-Road, e-identity) provides underlying infrastructure.
Current authoritative national AI strategy document and eesti.ai portal status need freshness verification.

sources.label: RIA Artificial Intelligence page (updated 2026-06-19)
sources.note: Current overview of Bürokratt, Aruait and AI cybersecurity work.

sources.label: Kratid AI support portfolio
sources.note: Institutional capacity-building support.

sources.label: Digital public services and AI readiness study (2023)
sources.note: Historical deployment inventory; 51 AI initiatives as of 2021 (stale).

entries.editorialSummary: Governance-and-inventory-first path: a binding statewide framework, mandatory intake, a public annual inventory, four risk tiers, pre-deployment impact assessments and an operating experimentation lab. Public evidence is unusually detailed for a state program, but the inventory is self-reported, includes broad productivity software and has not received a completed independent evaluation.

ai-strategy.note: Policy AI-01 remains the current statewide framework and is reinforced by the FY2026 IT Strategic Plan.

dedicated-governance.note: An operating AI Board, policy subcommittee and high-risk use-case subcommittee oversee policy and proposed uses.

staffing-budget.note: AI Fellows and an AI Area of Practice are documented; current dedicated headcount and program budget are not published.

internal-inventory.note: Agencies must submit in-scope AI before deployment, after material changes and during annual refreshes.

public-register.note: The 2025 public inventory displays 28 Connecticut-defined system rows and has machine-readable resources.

register-completeness.note: Annual accuracy and completeness review is mandatory, but public reconciliation against an internal inventory is not published.

transparency-labeling.note: The binding framework requires disclosure for external-facing AI content and service inputs or outputs and supplies notice text.

independent-evaluation.note: No completed independent public audit, academic evaluation or external statewide effectiveness assessment was located in the documented search.

public-facing-ai.note: The inventory includes resident-facing uses such as CareerConnect CT and the Department of Insurance website chatbot.

employee-ai.note: The inventory lists internal search, drafting, coding, service-desk and productivity tools; limited pilots remain labeled separately.

open-source.note: Public procedures, assessment standards and open-data formats are reusable; no state AI codebase or public AI API was verified.

adoption-metrics.note: Connecticut publishes 28 inventory rows and planned value measures, but not current users, sessions, service outcomes or validated savings.

ai-literacy.note: An AI Area of Practice trains staff, and the state reports launching Generative AI in the Public Sector training.

procurement-guidance.note: Binding due diligence requires BITS approval, impact assessment, vendor certification, explainability and supplier-training review.

data-infrastructure.note: A protected AI Enablement Lab and secure Microsoft-tenant Copilot environment are operationally described.

sandbox-participation.note: Agencies can use the protected AI Enablement Lab; the state also ran a limited ChatGPT pilot and explored more than 30 use cases.

risk-classification.note: Local analogue: low, medium, high and severe tiers trigger escalating review and human-control requirements.

obligation-inventory.note: Local analogue: the framework maps mandatory responsibilities across agencies, BITS, the AI Board and vendors.

article-49-readiness.note: Structurally not applicable to ordinary Connecticut state deployments; excluded from US comparison denominators.

fria-dpia.note: Local analogue: pre-implementation and annual impact assessment are mandatory, and all 28 displayed rows are officially marked impact assessed.

human-oversight.note: Binding risk tiers require human review or authority, including full human control for severe uses, but no uniform challenge or appeal route was verified.

monitoring-incidents.note: Annual reassessment covers inputs, outputs, logic, errors, accuracy and appropriateness, with vendor issue reporting and mitigation; no public incident log was found.

public-participation.note: The framework provides for external input and recommends involving residents with relevant lived experience; no completed change process was verified.

freshness-discipline.note: The inventory was updated 2025-12-08, material changes require resubmission and annual accuracy review is mandatory.

keyFindings: The 2025 public inventory displays 28 Connecticut-defined system rows and exposes machine-readable resources; the count is not directly comparable with European register counts.
Every displayed inventory row is officially marked impact assessed, but the flags are self-reported and do not establish assessment quality, publication or independence.
The binding framework contains operational disclosure language for AI-assisted content and services.
Resident-facing and employee-facing deployments are both evidenced, while pilots and broad commodity software remain labeled separately.
No completed independent evaluation of the statewide program was located.

sources.note: Operating AI Board and two subcommittees, dated 2025-12-08.

entries.editorialSummary: Governance-and-enablement-first path: a binding responsible-AI policy, statutory AI Subcabinet, versioned governance cards, risk-based intake, employee training and a controlled Innovation Lab. Governance evidence is current, but direct inspection of the public inventory endpoint and production use-case records remains incomplete.

ai-strategy.note: The 2025 AI Enablement Strategy remains tied to 2026 work and is backed by an operating statutory action-plan mandate.

dedicated-governance.note: The statutory AI Subcabinet, DoIT AI Enablement Team and agency AI leads have defined operating responsibilities.

staffing-budget.note: Maryland reports forming and hiring an AI Enablement Team; dedicated headcount and program budget are not published.

internal-inventory.note: Maryland collected a first statewide inventory and requires production use submissions and regular high-risk inventories.

public-register.note: Policy requires annual publication and refers to a public AI inventory, but the live entry endpoint, count and search behavior were not directly verified.

register-completeness.note: Agencies must submit production uses and high-risk inventories; no public reconciliation against all agency submissions was inspected.

transparency-labeling.note: Governance cards require citation for significantly AI-generated content, and policy routes disclosure requirements through intake and procurement.

independent-evaluation.note: No completed public audit, academic evaluation or external statewide effectiveness study was located in the documented search.

public-facing-ai.note: Official sources document proofs of concept and lab work in service domains; no named production resident-facing AI service was directly verified.

employee-ai.note: Gemini Workspace and government coding assistants are approved and agencies have experimented with GenAI; current adoption counts were not inspected.

open-source.note: Maryland publishes versioned governance cards and reusable standards; no released state AI codebase or public AI API was verified.

adoption-metrics.note: Intake requires success metrics, but current usage and independently supported outcome data are not published.

ai-literacy.note: Maryland made free AI training available and launched a statewide AI Community of Practice.

procurement-guidance.note: New AI procurement or deployment must comply with DoIT policy; governance cards add tool-specific supplier and configuration controls.

data-infrastructure.note: The Innovation Lab provides a controlled sandbox and software packages; approved coding access includes a managed AWS Bedrock route.

sandbox-participation.note: Maryland ran named proofs of concept and launched an operating Innovation Lab for agency experimentation.

risk-classification.note: Local analogue: implementation guidance applies repeatable risk categories and mandatory safeguards for high-risk uses.

obligation-inventory.note: Local analogue: statute and policy map responsibilities across the AI Subcabinet, DoIT, agencies and control functions.

article-49-readiness.note: Structurally not applicable to ordinary Maryland state deployments; excluded from US comparison denominators.

fria-dpia.note: Local analogue: high-risk proposals must complete AIA and privacy documentation before deployment, but no completed public assessment was inspected.

human-oversight.note: Policy requires human oversight and human-in-the-loop control for critical decisions, but no uniform challenge or appeal route was verified.

monitoring-incidents.note: Agencies must maintain incident-response plans, report AI incidents to DoIT and continuously monitor performance, errors, drift and risk; no public log was found.

public-participation.note: The strategy reports engagement with civil society, academia, industry and other governments; no specific governance change was traced to feedback.

freshness-discipline.note: The policy dates from 2025, coding guidance was revised in May 2026 and the Innovation Lab launched in May 2026.

keyFindings: The AI Subcabinet, DoIT and agency AI leads form a current legal and organizational chain with monitoring and compliance responsibilities.
The May 2026 Innovation Lab is operating infrastructure and sandbox evidence, not evidence that every prototype reached production.
Versioned governance cards specify controls for coding assistants and Gemini Workspace, including approved environments and disclosure rules.
Official materials refer to a public AI inventory, but its live endpoint and entries were not directly inspected; the public-register score remains announced.
Continuous monitoring and incident reporting are binding policy elements, while completed independent evaluation was not located.

entries.editorialSummary: Policy-and-platform-first path: a statewide GenAI executive order, binding inventory and procurement rules, and a state-built employee platform effective 2026-07-01 after a large pilot. The evidence supports current internal deployment of Poppy, but not a statewide public AI register, a named production resident-facing AI service or completed independent evaluation.

ai-strategy.note: Executive Order N-12-23 is implemented through current GenAI policy, procurement rules, the official portal and Poppy.

dedicated-governance.note: Statewide responsibilities are assigned across the Governor's Office, GovOps, CDT, DGS and executive-branch entities.

staffing-budget.note: Poppy was built by state employees and is operated by CDT; dedicated AI headcount and program budget were not verified.

internal-inventory.note: Executive-branch entities must identify, track and maintain GenAI uses and a high-risk inventory.

public-register.note: No searchable statewide public register with live state-government AI system entries was located in the documented official-source search.

register-completeness.note: No verified public register exists to reconcile against executive-branch internal inventories.

transparency-labeling.note: The accountability framework establishes transparency expectations; cross-agency operational interaction notices were not directly sampled.

independent-evaluation.note: No completed independent audit, academic evaluation or external statewide effectiveness assessment of the GenAI program or Poppy was located.

public-facing-ai.note: Statewide policy and pilot pathways exist; no named production resident-facing state AI service was directly verified. Poppy is employee-facing.

employee-ai.note: Poppy became effective 2026-07-01 and is available for departmental enrollment in a state-controlled environment.

open-source.note: California publishes reusable policy and procurement standards; Poppy code, public APIs and an open-source repository were not verified.

adoption-metrics.note: The pilot involved more than 2,800 employees across 67 departments; post-launch users, sessions and outcomes are not published.

ai-literacy.note: A current required or measured statewide employee training program was not directly verified.

procurement-guidance.note: Revised binding procurement rules apply across acquisition types and contract forms.

data-infrastructure.note: Poppy is a state-built, vendor-agnostic platform operating in a trusted state environment with enterprise security controls.

sandbox-participation.note: California completed a Poppy pilot involving 67 departments before statewide rollout.

risk-classification.note: Local analogue: the State Administrative Manual requires use, risk and impact identification and a high-risk GenAI inventory.

obligation-inventory.note: Local analogue: executive-order, administrative-manual and procurement responsibilities are allocated among central agencies, departments and contractors.

article-49-readiness.note: Structurally not applicable to ordinary California state deployments; excluded from US comparison denominators.

fria-dpia.note: Local analogue: high-risk identification and assessment processes are binding, but no completed system-specific public assessment was verified.

human-oversight.note: State policy establishes high-risk governance and human-accountability expectations; a named statewide challenge or redress process was not verified.

monitoring-incidents.note: Risk-management duties are evident, but a statewide AI-specific performance-monitoring, complaint and incident workflow was not directly verified.

public-participation.note: No qualifying evidence was located that public feedback changed state-government AI governance or a deployment; employee pilot feedback does not qualify.

freshness-discipline.note: Inventory and procurement rules were revised in February 2025; Poppy's launch alert was issued 2026-06-03 with a 2026-07-01 effective date.

keyFindings: Poppy is a deployed employee platform as of 2026-07-01; it is not a resident-facing service.
The Poppy pilot involved more than 2,800 employees across 67 departments, but those figures are not post-launch active-user metrics.
Executive-branch entities must maintain GenAI and high-risk inventories, but no searchable public register with system entries was verified.
Binding procurement controls are more directly evidenced than public transparency or completed public assessments.
No named production resident-facing service, completed public impact assessment or independent statewide evaluation was verified.

sources.label: Executive Order N-12-23
sources.note: Statewide GenAI governance, procurement, risk, training and pilot direction.

sources.label: GenAI Use Identification and High-Risk Inventory, SAM 4986.3
sources.note: Binding executive-branch identification and inventory policy.

sources.label: GenAI Procurement Policy and Procedures, SCM 23
sources.note: Binding procurement manual revised 2025-02.

sources.label: TA 26-04: Poppy, California's Digital Assistant
sources.note: Technology alert issued 2026-06-03 and effective 2026-07-01.

sources.label: Poppy: California's Digital Assistant
sources.note: State-built employee platform and pilot scale.

sources.label: California GenAI program portal
sources.note: Official program portal for statewide GenAI materials.

sources.label: SB 896 Generative Artificial Intelligence Accountability Act
sources.note: Statutory GenAI accountability framework.

source.label: KI-Assistenz F13 wird zur Open-Source-Software
source.note: Open-source release for reuse by federal, state and local governments.

framingSources.label: Oxford Insights, Government AI Readiness Index 2025
framingSources.note: Annual national-level readiness index covering 195 countries. No subnational coverage.

framingSources.label: Oxford Insights, Government AI Readiness Index 2024 methodology
framingSources.note: Methodology: published strategy / draft strategy / no evidence scored 100/50/0.

framingSources.label: OECD AI Index technical paper
framingSources.note: Composite framework covering policy, trust and AI-ecosystem enablers.

framingSources.label: Cath & Jansen (2021), Dutch Comfort: The limits of AI governance through municipal registers
framingSources.note: Argues Amsterdam and Helsinki registers contain limited sets and risk becoming ethics theatre if detached from political and welfare-state contexts.

framingSources.label: Nieuwenhuizen (2025), Algorithm Registers: A Box-Ticking Exercise or Meaningful Tool for Transparency?
framingSources.note: 27 interviews, 33 policy documents across Dutch registers and oversight bodies. Found competing views on register value.

framingSources.label: Stabilizing translucencies: Governing AI transparency by standardization (2024)
framingSources.note: Treats Amsterdam and Helsinki as examples of standardized disclosure structures.

framingSources.label: Eurocities Algorithmic Transparency Standard
framingSources.note: Disclosure vocabulary for system-level records; participating cities include Amsterdam, Rotterdam, Barcelona, Brussels, Mannheim.

framingSources.label: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689: EU Artificial Intelligence Act
framingSources.note: High-risk deployer obligations (Articles 26, 27, 49) largely apply from August 2, 2026.
